title: Enterprise Network Design Capstone
description: Apply comprehensive network design principles to a multi-site enterprise scenario, covering topology design, IP addressing, routing, security architecture, wireless planning, and cost estimation.
starterCode:
# ENTERPRISE NETWORK DESIGN CAPSTONE EXERCISE

## Part 1: Topology Design

1. A three-tier campus architecture consists of Core, _______, and Access layers.
2. For a small branch office with fewer than 100 users, a _______ core (two-tier) design is more cost-effective.
3. In a data center, a ______-leaf topology optimizes east-west traffic by providing equal-hop paths.
4. VSS (Virtual _______ System) allows two physical switches to operate as a single logical switch.
5. The Core layer provides high-speed _______ between distribution blocks and the WAN edge.
6. Access layer switches provide PoE+ for IP phones and wireless _______ points.
7. A _______ (Demilitarized Zone) segment isolates public-facing servers from the internal network.

## Part 2: IP Addressing and VLSM

8. RFC _______ defines private IPv4 address ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x).
9. Using /31 subnets (RFC _______) on point-to-point links conserves IP addresses by eliminating network/broadcast addresses.
10. VLSM stands for Variable Length _______ Masking, allowing different subnet sizes within the same network.
11. A /23 subnet provides _______ usable host addresses — enough for a 200-person department with growth room.
12. The native VLAN should be changed from VLAN _______ to a dedicated, unused VLAN for security.
13. Voice VLANs are given separate VLAN IDs to enable QoS marking at the _______ port level.
14. IoT devices should be placed in a separate, ______-segmented VLAN with restricted ACLs.

## Part 3: Routing Protocol Design

15. Multi-area OSPF limits LSA _______ by confining topology changes within an area.
16. Area _______ is the OSPF backbone that all other areas must connect to.
17. A _______ area in OSPF receives only a default route from the ABR, reducing LSDB size.
18. ABR stands for Area _______ Router, which connects two OSPF areas.
19. Router IDs should use _______ interfaces because they are always up and provide stability.
20. OSPF _______ authentication prevents rogue routers from forming adjacencies.
21. For WAN edge, eBGP provides internet redundancy by peering with multiple _______.

## Part 4: Security Architecture

22. Defense in _______ means layering multiple security controls so no single point of failure compromises the network.
23. A next-generation firewall (NGFW) performs _______ inspection — examining packet contents, not just headers.
24. 802.1X provides port-based network access _______ at the switch level.
25. NAC (Network _______ Control) checks device health before granting network access.
26. HIPAA §164.312(e) requires _______ security for electronic protected health information in transit.
27. TLS _______ is the latest version, featuring 1-RTT handshakes and mandatory forward secrecy.
28. A WAF (Web Application _______) protects HTTP/HTTPS applications from L7 attacks.

## Part 5: Wireless Design

29. To minimize co-channel interference on 2.4 GHz, use non-overlapping channels 1, _______, and 11.
30. WPA3-_______ uses 802.1X with EAP-TLS for certificate-based wireless authentication.
31. Each additional SSID broadcast on an AP consumes _______, reducing available throughput for clients.
32. 802.3bt (PoE++) provides up to _______ watts per port for high-power APs.
33. For high-density conference rooms, deploy APs using lower _______ power to create smaller cells.
34. The 6 GHz band available for Wi-Fi 6E/7 requires AFC (Automated _______ Coordination) in the US.
35. Limiting SSIDs to _______ or fewer per radio prevents excessive beacon overhead.

## Part 6: Monitoring and Cost

36. SNMPv3 adds _______, authentication, and encryption compared to SNMPv2c.
37. The _______ metric on a WAN link triggering a critical alert is typically 90% sustained for 5 minutes.
38. RTO stands for Recovery _______ Objective — the maximum acceptable time to restore service.
39. RPO stands for Recovery _______ Objective — the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
40. CapEx covers _______ expenditures (equipment purchase), while OpEx covers annual operational costs.
41. Professional _______ costs for installation should be included in the network design budget.
42. An HA pair of firewalls ensures _______ through automatic failover if one unit fails.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Topology Design

1. A three-tier campus architecture consists of Core, **Distribution**, and Access layers.
2. For a small branch office with fewer than 100 users, a **collapsed** core (two-tier) design is more cost-effective.
3. In a data center, a **spine**-leaf topology optimizes east-west traffic by providing equal-hop paths.
4. VSS (Virtual **Switching** System) allows two physical switches to operate as a single logical switch.
5. The Core layer provides high-speed **transport** between distribution blocks and the WAN edge.
6. Access layer switches provide PoE+ for IP phones and wireless **access** points.
7. A **DMZ** (Demilitarized Zone) segment isolates public-facing servers from the internal network.

## Part 2: IP Addressing and VLSM

8. RFC **1918** defines private IPv4 address ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x).
9. Using /31 subnets (RFC **3021**) on point-to-point links conserves IP addresses by eliminating network/broadcast addresses.
10. VLSM stands for Variable Length **Subnet** Masking, allowing different subnet sizes within the same network.
11. A /23 subnet provides **510** usable host addresses — enough for a 200-person department with growth room.
12. The native VLAN should be changed from VLAN **1** to a dedicated, unused VLAN for security.
13. Voice VLANs are given separate VLAN IDs to enable QoS marking at the **switch** port level.
14. IoT devices should be placed in a separate, **micro**-segmented VLAN with restricted ACLs.

## Part 3: Routing Protocol Design

15. Multi-area OSPF limits LSA **flooding** by confining topology changes within an area.
16. Area **0** is the OSPF backbone that all other areas must connect to.
17. A **stub** area in OSPF receives only a default route from the ABR, reducing LSDB size.
18. ABR stands for Area **Border** Router, which connects two OSPF areas.
19. Router IDs should use **loopback** interfaces because they are always up and provide stability.
20. OSPF **MD5** authentication prevents rogue routers from forming adjacencies.
21. For WAN edge, eBGP provides internet redundancy by peering with multiple **ISPs**.

## Part 4: Security Architecture

22. Defense in **depth** means layering multiple security controls so no single point of failure compromises the network.
23. A next-generation firewall (NGFW) performs **deep** inspection — examining packet contents, not just headers.
24. 802.1X provides port-based network access **control** at the switch level.
25. NAC (Network **Access** Control) checks device health before granting network access.
26. HIPAA §164.312(e) requires **transmission** security for electronic protected health information in transit.
27. TLS **1.3** is the latest version, featuring 1-RTT handshakes and mandatory forward secrecy.
28. A WAF (Web Application **Firewall**) protects HTTP/HTTPS applications from L7 attacks.

## Part 5: Wireless Design

29. To minimize co-channel interference on 2.4 GHz, use non-overlapping channels 1, **6**, and 11.
30. WPA3-**Enterprise** uses 802.1X with EAP-TLS for certificate-based wireless authentication.
31. Each additional SSID broadcast on an AP consumes **airtime**, reducing available throughput for clients.
32. 802.3bt (PoE++) provides up to **90** watts per port for high-power APs.
33. For high-density conference rooms, deploy APs using lower **transmit** power to create smaller cells.
34. The 6 GHz band available for Wi-Fi 6E/7 requires AFC (Automated **Frequency** Coordination) in the US.
35. Limiting SSIDs to **3** or fewer per radio prevents excessive beacon overhead.

## Part 6: Monitoring and Cost

36. SNMPv3 adds **privacy**, authentication, and encryption compared to SNMPv2c.
37. The **utilization** metric on a WAN link triggering a critical alert is typically 90% sustained for 5 minutes.
38. RTO stands for Recovery **Time** Objective — the maximum acceptable time to restore service.
39. RPO stands for Recovery **Point** Objective — the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
40. CapEx covers **capital** expenditures (equipment purchase), while OpEx covers annual operational costs.
41. Professional **services** costs for installation should be included in the network design budget.
42. An HA pair of firewalls ensures **availability** through automatic failover if one unit fails.
hints: The three-tier model uses Core (high-speed transport), Distribution (policy/routing), and Access (user ports).
Collapsed core (two-tier) combines Core and Distribution for smaller sites (<100 users).
Spine-leaf is ideal for data centers; every server is the same number of hops from every other server.
RFC 1918 defines private IPs; RFC 3021 defines /31 subnets for point-to-point links.
A /23 provides 2^9 - 2 = 510 usable hosts; VLSM = Variable Length Subnet Masking.
OSPF Area 0 is the backbone; stub areas only receive a default route to reduce LSDB size.
ABR = Area Border Router; MD5 authentication prevents unauthorized OSPF adjacencies.
Defense in depth layers controls; 802.1X provides port-based access control; TLS 1.3 is the latest.
RTO = Recovery Time Objective; RPO = Recovery Point Objective; CapEx = Capital Expenditures.
